light_pressure_read.read: High pressure and low cloud cover make this fishable but technical.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

caution_notes: Bright/high-pressure conditions may make fish cover-oriented and less forgiving.

generated_text.quick_read: Trout fishing on the Toccoa River is fair with recent stocking boosting activity. High pressure and bright conditions will make the fish a bit tricky but still catchable with careful tactics.
generated_text.best_window: The day 2 morning window is best because high pressure and low cloud cover create technical conditions where trout favor shaded, deeper runs and structure, matching the recommended cautious presentation style.
generated_text.current_conditions: Current USGS flow is 236 cfs with a gage height of 1.23 ft. Water temperature and turbidity data are unavailable.
generated_text.weather_setup: Current conditions include 27% average sky cover, high pressure at 30.2 inHg, steady with no clear trend, light wind averaging 1.5 mph, and a 29% chance of light rain expected later.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout will stay close to cover such as shade, deeper runs, undercut banks, and structure, feeding cautiously in bright, high-pressure conditions. Use smaller natural flies and stocked-trout attractors with careful, less aggressive drifts to entice strikes, keeping in mind fish may be cover-oriented and less willing to move.
